html:
𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄

THE ANCIENT COVENANT RECORD

Volume Nine — Part Five

The Book of Tehillim

TEHILLIM

The Psalms — The Praises of Yisra'EL

Psalms 90 through 106

Book IV — The Songs of YHWH's Eternal Reign

PRIMARY MANUSCRIPT AUTHORITIES

11Q5 (11QPsa) — Cave 11 — Major Psalms Scroll — primary source for Book IV

4Q83 (4QPsa) — Cave 4 — Psalms 5-71 fragments

4Q84 (4QPsb) — Cave 4 — Psalms 91-118

2Q14 (2QPsa) — Cave 2 — Psalms 103-104

Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — Complete Canon — All 150 Psalms plus Psalm 151

COMPARATIVE NOTES AT END OF EVERY CHAPTER

DSS · ORIT GE'EZ · MASORETIC VARIANT · CRITICAL NOTE

YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 and The Creator only — no substitute titles

All personal names in original Hebrew form throughout

BOOK IV — PSALMS 90 THROUGH 106 — COMPLETE

TEHILLIM — PSALM 90 A Prayer of Moshe the Man of Creator — From Everlasting to Everlasting You Are Creator

1 YHWH, you have been our dwelling place in all generations.

2 Before the mountains were brought forth, or ever you had formed the earth and the world, from everlasting to everlasting you are Creator.

3 You return man to dust and say, Return, O children of man.

4 For a thousand years in your sight are but as yesterday when it is past, or as a watch in the night.

5 You sweep them away as with a flood — they are like a dream, like grass that is renewed in the morning.

6 In the morning it flourishes and is renewed — in the evening it fades and withers.

7 For we are brought to an end by your anger — by your wrath we are dismayed.

8 You have set our iniquities before you, our secret sins in the light of your presence.

9 For all our days pass away under your wrath — we bring our years to an end like a sigh.

10 The years of our life are seventy, or even by reason of strength eighty — yet their span is but toil and trouble — they are soon gone, and we fly away.

11 Who considers the power of your anger, and your wrath according to the fear of you?

12 So teach us to number our days that we may get a heart of wisdom.

13 Return, O YHWH — how long? Have pity on your servants.

14 Satisfy us in the morning with your steadfast love, that we may rejoice and be glad all our days.

15 Make us glad for as many days as you have afflicted us, and for as many years as we have seen evil.

16 Let your work be shown to your servants, and your glorious power to their children.

17 Let the favor of YHWH our Creator be upon us, and establish the work of our hands upon us — yes, establish the work of our hands.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 90 opens Book IV and is the only psalm attributed to Moshe. It is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 90 in full. The psalm is read as Moshe's covenant meditation on human transience before YHWH's eternity — the prayer of the man who stood closest to YHWH of any generation.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 90. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — from everlasting to everlasting you are Creator. YHWH's eternity frames the entire psalm — before the mountains, before the earth, YHWH was. Verse 4 — a thousand years in your sight are but as yesterday. The asymmetry between divine and human time is the theological ground of the psalm. Verse 12 — teach us to number our days that we may get a heart of wisdom. The numbering of days is not calculation but orientation — the awareness of finitude producing the covenant wisdom of living each day before YHWH. Verse 17 — establish the work of our hands. The prayer closes with a petition for lasting significance — that the brief human life invested in YHWH's work would endure beyond the individual's span.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 91 He Who Dwells in the Shelter of the Most High — Under His Wings You Will Find Refuge

1 He who dwells in the shelter of the Most High will abide in the shadow of the Almighty.

2 I will say to YHWH, My refuge and my fortress, my Creator, in whom I trust.

3 For he will deliver you from the snare of the fowler and from the deadly pestilence.

4 He will cover you with his pinions, and under his wings you will find refuge — his faithfulness is a shield and buckler.

5 You will not fear the terror of the night, nor the arrow that flies by day,

6 Nor the pestilence that stalks in darkness, nor the destruction that wastes at noonday.

7 A thousand may fall at your side, ten thousand at your right hand, but it will not come near you.

8 You will only look with your eyes and see the recompense of the wicked.

9 Because you have made YHWH your dwelling place — the Most High, who is my refuge —

10 No evil shall be allowed to befall you, no plague come near your tent.

11 For he will command his angels concerning you to guard you in all your ways.

12 On their hands they will bear you up, lest you strike your foot against a stone.

13 You will tread on the lion and the adder — the young lion and the serpent you will trample underfoot.

14 Because he holds fast to me in love, I will deliver him — I will protect him, because he knows my name.

15 When he calls to me, I will answer him — I will be with him in trouble — I will rescue him and honor him.

16 With long life I will satisfy him and show him my salvation.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 91 is attested in 4QPsa (4Q83) and most significantly in 11QPsa (11Q5) — the major Cave 11 Psalms Scroll. The Cave 11 manuscript is particularly well preserved for this psalm. Verse 11 — he will command his angels concerning you — is among the most cited verses from Tehillim in the DSS corpus, appearing in contexts of angelic protection theology.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 91 in full. The psalm is one of the most beloved in the Beta Israel tradition, used as a prayer of protection and covenant trust in times of danger.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 91. The Cave 11 and Cave 4 manuscripts align with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 11 — he will command his angels concerning you to guard you in all your ways. The angelic guardianship of the covenant person is the same theology as the War Scroll's angelic participants in battle — Chanokh's Watchers and holy ones — and the Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice. The angels are not distant observers but active guardians of those who know YHWH's name — verse 14. Verse 14-16 are remarkable: YHWH himself speaks directly in the psalm. The promise is covenant: because he holds fast to me in love, because he knows my name — I will deliver, protect, answer, be with, rescue, honor, satisfy. The covenant relationship is the basis of every promise in these verses.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 92 A Psalm — A Song for the Sabbath — It Is Good to Give Thanks to YHWH

1 It is good to give thanks to YHWH, to sing praises to your name, O Most High,

2 To declare your steadfast love in the morning, and your faithfulness by night,

3 To the music of the lute and the harp, to the melody of the lyre.

4 For you, O YHWH, have made me glad by your work — at the works of your hands I sing for joy.

5 How great are your works, O YHWH — your thoughts are very deep.

6 The stupid man cannot know — the fool cannot understand this:

7 That though the wicked sprout like grass and all evildoers flourish, they are doomed to destruction forever.

8 But you, O YHWH, are on high forever.

9 For behold, your enemies, O YHWH — for behold, your enemies shall perish — all evildoers shall be scattered.

10 But you have exalted my horn like that of the wild ox — you have poured over me fresh oil.

11 My eye has seen the downfall of my enemies — my ears have heard the doom of my evil assailants.

12 The righteous flourish like the palm tree and grow like a cedar in Lebanon.

13 They are planted in the house of YHWH — they flourish in the courts of our Creator.

14 They still bear fruit in old age — they are ever full of sap and green,

15 To declare that YHWH is upright — he is my rock, and there is no unrighteousness in him.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 92 is the only psalm in the Tehillim explicitly designated for the Sabbath. It is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) and in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The Qumran community's commitment to the Sabbath calendar makes this psalm significant in their liturgical life. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 92 in full. The Sabbath designation connects the psalm to the Beta Israel community's Sabbath observance — one of the most foundational practices in the Ethiopian Israelite tradition.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 92. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12-14 — the righteous flourish like the palm tree and grow like a cedar in Lebanon — they are planted in the house of YHWH — they still bear fruit in old age. The covenant person does not diminish with age — verse 14 declares that even in old age they are full of sap and green. This is the covenant counterpart to Psalm 90's meditation on human brevity: those rooted in YHWH's house bear fruit to the end. Verse 15 — to declare that YHWH is upright — he is my rock, and there is no unrighteousness in him. The fruit borne in old age is testimony to YHWH's character.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 93 YHWH Reigns — He Is Robed in Majesty — Your Throne Is Established From of Old

1 YHWH reigns — he is robed in majesty — YHWH is robed — he has put on strength as his belt. Yes, the world is established — it shall never be moved.

2 Your throne is established from of old — you are from everlasting.

3 The floods have lifted up, O YHWH — the floods have lifted up their voice — the floods lift up their roaring.

4 Mightier than the thunders of many waters, mightier than the waves of the sea, YHWH on high is mighty.

5 Your decrees are very trustworthy — holiness befits your house, O YHWH, forevermore.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 93 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is one of the enthronement psalms — YHWH reigns — a brief but theologically dense declaration of divine sovereignty over the cosmos. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 93 in full. The declaration YHWH reigns — verse 1 — is read as the foundational covenant affirmation that underlies all of the Tehillim's praise.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 93. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 1 — YHWH reigns — he is robed in majesty — he has put on strength as his belt. The enthronement language of Psalm 93 echoes throughout the Tehillim and the prophetic corpus: YHWH as divine warrior-king, clothed in the attributes of sovereignty. Verse 3-4 — the floods lift up their roaring — mightier than the thunders of many waters, mightier than the waves of the sea, YHWH on high is mighty. The forces of cosmic chaos — the flooding waters, the roaring sea — cannot stand before YHWH's might. The same imagery of YHWH taming the deep appears in Psalms 29, 46, 74, and the creation account of Bereshit 1.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 94 O Creator of Vengeance — How Long Shall the Wicked Triumph — YHWH Will Not Forsake His People

1 O Creator of vengeance, O Creator of vengeance, shine forth.

2 Rise up, O judge of the earth — repay to the proud what they deserve.

3 O YHWH, how long shall the wicked, how long shall the wicked exult?

4 They pour out their arrogant words — all the evildoers boast.

5 They crush your people, O YHWH, and afflict your heritage.

6 They kill the widow and the sojourner, and murder the fatherless.

7 And they say, YHWH does not see — the Creator of Yaakov does not perceive.

8 Understand, O dullest of the people — fools, when will you be wise?

9 He who planted the ear, does he not hear? He who formed the eye, does he not see?

10 He who disciplines the nations, does he not rebuke? He who teaches man knowledge —

11 YHWH knows the thoughts of man, that they are but a breath.

12 Blessed is the man whom you discipline, O YHWH, and whom you teach out of your Torah,

13 To give him rest from days of trouble, until a pit is dug for the wicked.

14 For YHWH will not forsake his people — he will not abandon his heritage.

15 For justice will return to the righteous, and all the upright in heart will follow it.

16 Who rises up for me against the wicked? Who stands up for me against evildoers?

17 If YHWH had not been my help, my soul would soon have lived in the land of silence.

18 When I thought, My foot slips, your steadfast love, O YHWH, held me up.

19 When the cares of my heart are many, your consolations cheer my soul.

20 Can wicked rulers be allied with you, those who frame injustice by statute?

21 They band together against the life of the righteous and condemn the innocent to death.

22 But YHWH has become my stronghold, and my Creator the rock of my refuge.

23 He will bring back on them their iniquity and wipe them out for their wickedness — YHWH our Creator will wipe them out.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 94 is attested in fragmentary form in the Cave 4 Psalms manuscripts. The psalm calls for YHWH's judgment against rulers who frame injustice by statute — a direct challenge to the misuse of law for oppression. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 94 in full. The psalm is read as the covenant community's cry against systemic injustice — those who kill the widow, sojourner, and fatherless while claiming YHWH does not see.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 94. The Masoretic text aligns with the DSS reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 9 — he who planted the ear, does he not hear? He who formed the eye, does he not see? The argument from creation: the Creator of the faculty cannot lack the faculty. YHWH who made the human ear hears everything. Verse 20 — can wicked rulers be allied with you, those who frame injustice by statute? The covenant indictment of legal systems that institutionalize oppression is one of the sharpest in the Tehillim. Injustice encoded in law is still injustice. YHWH is not allied with courts that condemn the innocent.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 95 Oh Come, Let Us Sing to YHWH — Do Not Harden Your Hearts as at Merivah

1 Oh come, let us sing to YHWH — let us make a joyful noise to the rock of our salvation.

2 Let us come into his presence with thanksgiving — let us make a joyful noise to him with songs of praise.

3 For YHWH is a great Creator, and a great King above all gods.

4 In his hand are the depths of the earth — the heights of the mountains are his also.

5 The sea is his, for he made it, and his hands formed the dry land.

6 Oh come, let us worship and bow down — let us kneel before YHWH, our Maker.

7 For he is our Creator, and we are the people of his pasture, and the sheep of his hand. Today, if you hear his voice,

8 Do not harden your hearts, as at Merivah, as on the day at Massah in the wilderness,

9 When your fathers put me to the test and put me to the proof, though they had seen my work.

10 For forty years I loathed that generation and said, They are a people who go astray in their heart, and they have not known my ways.

11 Therefore I swore in my wrath, They shall not enter my rest.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 95 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is a call to worship followed by a divine warning — the Qumran community interpreted the rest of verse 11 eschatologically as the covenant rest that still awaited the faithful. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 95 in full. The psalm is read as the perpetual covenant warning: the generation of the wilderness hardened their hearts and lost the rest. Each generation faces the same choice.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 95. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading. The Qumran community's commentary on this psalm — reflected in the Damascus Document — interprets the rest of verse 11 as not yet entered, awaiting the faithful remnant.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7-8 — today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts. The word today carries the entire weight of covenant urgency. Not yesterday's obedience or tomorrow's intention — today. The generation that hardened at Merivah did not lack knowledge of YHWH — verse 9, they had seen his work. They hardened despite knowing. Verse 11 — they shall not enter my rest. The covenant rest — the shabbat rest and the land rest — is forfeited by the hardened heart. The Qumran community read this as an ongoing warning for their own generation.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 96 Oh Sing to YHWH a New Song — Declare His Glory Among the Nations

1 Oh sing to YHWH a new song — sing to YHWH, all the earth.

2 Sing to YHWH, bless his name — tell of his salvation from day to day.

3 Declare his glory among the nations, his marvelous works among all the peoples.

4 For great is YHWH, and greatly to be praised — he is to be feared above all gods.

5 For all the gods of the peoples are worthless idols, but YHWH made the heavens.

6 Splendor and majesty are before him — strength and beauty are in his sanctuary.

7 Ascribe to YHWH, O families of the peoples, ascribe to YHWH glory and strength.

8 Ascribe to YHWH the glory due his name — bring an offering and come into his courts.

9 Worship YHWH in the splendor of holiness — tremble before him, all the earth.

10 Say among the nations, YHWH reigns — yes, the world is established — it shall never be moved — he will judge the peoples with equity.

11 Let the heavens be glad, and let the earth rejoice — let the sea roar, and all that fills it.

12 Let the field exult, and everything in it — then shall all the trees of the forest sing for joy

13 Before YHWH, for he comes, for he comes to judge the earth. He will judge the world in righteousness, and the peoples in his faithfulness.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 96 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11 and also appears in 1 Divrei HaYamim 16:23-33, confirming its liturgical use in temple worship. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 96 in full. The universal call to all nations and all creation — heavens, earth, sea, field, trees — to praise YHWH is read as the eschatological vision of universal worship.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The parallel in 1 Divrei HaYamim 16:23-33 shows minor variations from the Psalm 96 version, confirming independent transmission. The DSS Cave 11 Psalms Scroll preserves the Psalm 96 version as the primary liturgical text.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 5 — all the gods of the peoples are worthless idols, but YHWH made the heavens. The contrast is absolute: the gods are elilim — nothings, mere images — while YHWH is the Creator of the heavens. Verse 13 — he comes to judge the earth. He will judge the world in righteousness, and the peoples in his faithfulness. The coming of YHWH to judge is the ground of creation's joy in verses 11-12. The trees sing, the sea roars, the field exults — because YHWH's righteous judgment is coming. Creation longs for the covenant justice that will set all things right.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 97 YHWH Reigns — Let the Earth Rejoice — He Guards the Lives of His Saints

1 YHWH reigns, let the earth rejoice — let the many coastlands be glad.

2 Clouds and thick darkness are all around him — righteousness and justice are the foundation of his throne.

3 Fire goes before him and burns up his adversaries all around.

4 His lightnings light up the world — the earth sees and trembles.

5 The mountains melt like wax before YHWH, before the YHWH of all the earth.

6 The heavens proclaim his righteousness, and all the peoples see his glory.

7 All worshipers of images are put to shame, who make their boast in worthless idols — worship him, all you gods.

8 Tziyon hears and is glad, and the daughters of Yehudah rejoice, because of your judgments, O YHWH.

9 For you, O YHWH, are most high over all the earth — you are exalted far above all gods.

10 O you who love YHWH, hate evil — he preserves the lives of his saints — he delivers them from the hand of the wicked.

11 Light is sown for the righteous, and joy for the upright in heart.

12 Rejoice in YHWH, O you righteous, and give thanks to his holy name.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 97 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is an enthronement psalm — YHWH reigns — with a theophany description of fire, lightning, and melting mountains that echoes the Sinai revelation. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 97 in full. The theophany imagery — clouds and thick darkness, fire, lightning — is read in direct connection with the Sinai revelation of Shemot 19 and the theophany of Chanokh 1.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 97. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — clouds and thick darkness are all around him — righteousness and justice are the foundation of his throne. The inaccessible mystery of YHWH — the dark cloud — and the ethical foundation of his sovereignty — righteousness and justice — are declared in the same breath. YHWH is not merely powerful but righteous. His throne rests on justice. Verse 10 — O you who love YHWH, hate evil. The love of YHWH has a moral consequence: the hatred of evil. The covenant person cannot love YHWH while embracing what YHWH hates. Verse 11 — light is sown for the righteous. The light is planted like seed — it will come up in its time.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 98 Oh Sing to YHWH a New Song — He Has Done Marvelous Things — All the Earth Has Seen His Victory

1 Oh sing to YHWH a new song, for he has done marvelous things — his right hand and his holy arm have worked salvation for him.

2 YHWH has made known his salvation — he has revealed his righteousness in the sight of the nations.

3 He has remembered his steadfast love and faithfulness to the house of Yisra'EL — all the ends of the earth have seen the salvation of our Creator.

4 Make a joyful noise to YHWH, all the earth — break forth into joyous song and sing praises.

5 Sing praises to YHWH with the lyre, with the lyre and the sound of melody.

6 With trumpets and the sound of the horn make a joyful noise before the King, YHWH.

7 Let the sea roar, and all that fills it — the world and those who dwell in it.

8 Let the rivers clap their hands — let the hills sing for joy together

9 Before YHWH, for he comes to judge the earth. He will judge the world with righteousness, and the peoples with equity.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 98 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is structurally similar to Psalm 96 and forms part of the enthronement psalm collection in Book IV. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 98 in full. Verse 3 — he has remembered his steadfast love and faithfulness to the house of Yisra'EL — is read as the covenant declaration that YHWH's acts of salvation are the fulfillment of his ancient covenant commitments.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 98. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8 — let the rivers clap their hands — let the hills sing for joy together. Creation participates in the praise of YHWH. Rivers applaud. Hills rejoice. This is not poetry for its own sake — it is covenant theology: YHWH's justice coming to judge the earth is the event that all creation has been waiting for. The restoration of righteous order is the completion of creation's purpose. Verse 3 — all the ends of the earth have seen the salvation of our Creator. YHWH's salvation of Yisra'EL is a public, visible act — seen by all nations — confirming his covenant faithfulness before the whole world.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 99 YHWH Reigns — He Is Holy — He Spoke to Them in the Pillar of Cloud

1 YHWH reigns — let the peoples tremble — he sits enthroned upon the cherubim — let the earth quake.

2 YHWH is great in Tziyon — he is exalted over all the peoples.

3 Let them praise your great and awesome name — Holy is he.

4 The King in his might loves justice — you have established equity — you have executed justice and righteousness in Yaakov.

5 Exalt YHWH our Creator — worship at his footstool — Holy is he.

6 Moshe and Aharon were among his priests, Shemu'el also was among those who called upon his name. They called to YHWH, and he answered them.

7 In the pillar of cloud he spoke to them — they kept his testimonies and the statute that he gave them.

8 O YHWH our Creator, you answered them — you were a forgiving Creator to them, but an avenger of their wrongdoings.

9 Exalt YHWH our Creator, and worship at his holy mountain — for YHWH our Creator is holy.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 99 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is the third of the enthronement psalms in Book IV — YHWH reigns — and names Moshe, Aharon, and Shemu'el as the paradigmatic covenant intercessors. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 99 in full. The naming of Moshe, Aharon, and Shemu'el as those who called upon YHWH and were answered is read as the covenant tradition of priestly intercession preserved in the Beta Israel community.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 99. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 7 — in the pillar of cloud he spoke to them — they kept his testimonies and the statute that he gave them. The covenant transmission from YHWH through the pillar of cloud — the same cloud that led Yisra'EL in the wilderness of Shemot 13 — is the paradigm of covenant obedience: YHWH speaks, the covenant people keep what he gave. Verse 8 — you were a forgiving Creator to them, but an avenger of their wrongdoings. YHWH's forgiveness and his justice coexist. He forgave Moshe, Aharon, and Shemu'el their failures while still holding them accountable. The covenant is not lenient — it is both merciful and just.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 100 A Psalm for Giving Thanks — Make a Joyful Noise to YHWH, All the Earth — His Steadfast Love Endures Forever

1 Make a joyful noise to YHWH, all the earth.

2 Serve YHWH with gladness — come into his presence with singing.

3 Know that YHWH, he is Creator — it is he who made us, and we are his — we are his people, and the sheep of his pasture.

4 Enter his gates with thanksgiving, and his courts with praise — give thanks to him — bless his name.

5 For YHWH is good — his steadfast love endures forever, and his faithfulness to all generations.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 100 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is a processional thanksgiving — the worshiper entering the gates and courts of YHWH's sanctuary with praise. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 100 in full. Verse 5 — YHWH is good, his steadfast love endures forever — is one of the most foundational covenant declarations in the Beta Israel liturgical tradition.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 100. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 3 — know that YHWH, he is Creator — it is he who made us, and we are his. The knowledge required is not intellectual assent but covenant recognition: YHWH alone is Creator, and his claim on humanity flows from his act of making. We are his because he made us and because we are the sheep of his pasture — both creation and covenant ground the relationship. Verse 5 — his steadfast love endures forever, and his faithfulness to all generations. The two attributes of YHWH's covenantal character — hesed and emet — are permanent. They outlast every human generation.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 101 Of Dawid — A Psalm — I Will Walk With Integrity of Heart — A Blameless Life

1 I will sing of steadfast love and justice — to you, O YHWH, I will make music.

2 I will ponder the way that is blameless. Oh when will you come to me? I will walk with integrity of heart within my house.

3 I will not set before my eyes anything that is worthless. I hate the work of those who fall away — it shall not cling to me.

4 A perverse heart shall be far from me — I will know nothing of evil.

5 Whoever slanders his neighbor secretly I will destroy. Whoever has a haughty look and an arrogant heart I will not endure.

6 I will look with favor on the faithful in the land, that they may dwell with me — he who walks in the way that is blameless shall minister to me.

7 No one who practices deceit shall dwell in my house — no one who utters lies shall continue before my eyes.

8 Morning by morning I will destroy all the wicked in the land, cutting off all the evildoers from the city of YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 101 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The psalm is a royal covenant of integrity — the king committing to a blameless life in his house and his governance. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 101 in full. The psalm is read as the covenant standard for leadership — the ruler who walks in integrity of heart and will not endure the arrogant or the deceitful in his household or governance.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 101. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 2 — I will walk with integrity of heart within my house. The covenant commitment begins in the house — the private sphere. The king's public righteousness is grounded in private integrity. What he commits to within his house shapes what he does in the city of YHWH — verse 8. Verse 3 — I will not set before my eyes anything that is worthless. The eye gate is the entry point of the worthless. What the covenant person looks at shapes what he becomes. The Qumran community's discipline of the eyes — regulating what members looked at — is the same principle applied institutionally.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 102 A Prayer of One Afflicted, When He Is Faint and Pours Out His Complaint Before YHWH — Do Not Hide Your Face in the Day of My Distress

1 Hear my prayer, O YHWH — let my cry come to you.

2 Do not hide your face from me in the day of my distress — incline your ear to me — answer me speedily in the day when I call.

3 For my days pass away like smoke, and my bones burn like a furnace.

4 My heart is struck down like grass and has withered — I forget to eat my bread.

5 Because of my loud groaning my bones cling to my flesh.

6 I am like a desert owl of the wilderness, like an owl of the waste places.

7 I lie awake — I am like a lonely sparrow on the housetop.

8 All the day my enemies taunt me — those who deride me use my name for a curse.

9 For I eat ashes like bread and mingle tears with my drink,

10 Because of your indignation and anger — for you have taken me up and thrown me down.

11 My days are like an evening shadow — I wither away like grass.

12 But you, O YHWH, are enthroned forever — you are remembered throughout all generations.

13 You will arise and have pity on Tziyon — it is the time to favor her — the appointed time has come.

14 For your servants hold her stones dear and have pity on her dust.

15 Nations will fear the name of YHWH, and all the kings of the earth will fear your glory.

16 For YHWH builds up Tziyon — he appears in his glory.

17 He regards the prayer of the destitute and does not despise their prayer.

18 Let this be recorded for a generation to come, so that a people yet to be created may praise YHWH.

19 That he looked down from his holy height — from heaven YHWH looked at the earth,

20 To hear the groans of the prisoners, to set free those who were doomed to die,

21 That they may declare in Tziyon the name of YHWH, and in Yerushalaym his praise,

22 When peoples gather together, and kingdoms, to worship YHWH.

23 He has broken my strength in midcourse — he has shortened my days.

24 I say, O my Creator, do not take me away in the midst of my days — you whose years endure throughout all generations.

25 Of old you laid the foundation of the earth, and the heavens are the work of your hands.

26 They will perish, but you will remain — they will all wear out like a garment. You will change them like a robe, and they will pass away,

27 But you are the same, and your years have no end.

28 The children of your servants shall dwell secure — their offspring shall be established before you.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 102 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11 and in fragmentary form in 4QPsb (4Q84). The psalm moves from individual lament to cosmic hope — the destitute person's prayer becomes the occasion for a declaration of YHWH's eternal sovereignty. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 102 in full. Verses 25-27 — you laid the foundation of the earth — they will perish but you will remain — you are the same and your years have no end — are among the most profound declarations of YHWH's immutability in the entire Tehillim.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 102. The Cave 11 and Cave 4 manuscripts align with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 26-27 — they will perish, but you will remain — they will all wear out like a garment — but you are the same, and your years have no end. The entire created order — heavens and earth — is transient. YHWH alone is permanent. The same Creator who laid their foundations will change them like a worn garment. Only YHWH does not change — he is the same. This is the covenant foundation for all trust: the God who made the covenant is the God who cannot change. His steadfast love endures precisely because he endures.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 103 Of Dawid — Bless YHWH, O My Soul — He Forgives All Your Iniquity — As Far as the East Is From the West

1 Bless YHWH, O my soul, and all that is within me, bless his holy name.

2 Bless YHWH, O my soul, and forget not all his benefits,

3 Who forgives all your iniquity, who heals all your diseases,

4 Who redeems your life from the pit, who crowns you with steadfast love and mercy,

5 Who satisfies you with good so that your youth is renewed like the eagle's.

6 YHWH works righteousness and justice for all who are oppressed.

7 He made known his ways to Moshe, his acts to the people of Yisra'EL.

8 YHWH is merciful and gracious, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love.

9 He will not always chide, nor will he keep his anger forever.

10 He does not deal with us according to our sins, nor repay us according to our iniquities.

11 For as high as the heavens are above the earth, so great is his steadfast love toward those who fear him.

12 As far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions from us.

13 As a father shows compassion to his children, so YHWH shows compassion to those who fear him.

14 For he knows our frame — he remembers that we are dust.

15 As for man, his days are like grass — he flourishes like a flower of the field.

16 For the wind passes over it, and it is gone, and its place knows it no more.

17 But the steadfast love of YHWH is from everlasting to everlasting on those who fear him, and his righteousness to children's children,

18 To those who keep his covenant and remember to do his commandments.

19 YHWH has established his throne in the heavens, and his kingdom rules over all.

20 Bless YHWH, O you his angels, you mighty ones who do his word, obeying the voice of his word.

21 Bless YHWH, all his hosts, his ministers, who do his will.

22 Bless YHWH, all his works, in all places of his dominion. Bless YHWH, O my soul.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 103 is attested in 2QPsa (2Q14) from Cave 2 and in fragmentary form in 4QPsb (4Q84). The Cave 2 manuscript is one of the smaller DSS witnesses and its preservation of portions of Psalm 103 confirms the psalm's wide liturgical use. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 103 in full. Verse 8 — YHWH is merciful and gracious, slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love — is the direct quotation of YHWH's self-declaration at Sinai in Shemot 34:6, foundational to the Beta Israel liturgical tradition.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 103. The Cave 2 and Cave 4 manuscripts align with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 12 — as far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions from us. The removal of transgressions is spatial — placed at infinite distance. Not buried, not covered temporarily, not merely forgiven in the ledger — removed, as far as the east is from the west, which is a distance with no measurable boundary. Verse 14 — he knows our frame — he remembers that we are dust. YHWH's compassion is grounded in his knowledge of what he made. He does not demand what dust cannot give. The same Creator who formed man from the dust — Bereshit 2:7 — remembers what man is made of.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 104 Bless YHWH, O My Soul — The Great Hymn of Creation

1 Bless YHWH, O my soul — O YHWH my Creator, you are very great — you are clothed with splendor and majesty,

2 Covering yourself with light as with a garment, stretching out the heavens like a tent.

3 He lays the beams of his chambers on the waters — he makes the clouds his chariot — he rides on the wings of the wind.

4 He makes his messengers winds, his ministers a flaming fire.

5 He set the earth on its foundations, so that it should never be moved.

6 You covered it with the deep as with a garment — the waters stood above the mountains.

7 At your rebuke they fled — at the sound of your thunder they took to flight.

8 The mountains rose, the valleys sank down to the place that you appointed for them.

9 You set a boundary that they may not pass, so that they might not again cover the earth.

10 You make springs gush forth in the valleys — they flow between the hills.

11 They give drink to every beast of the field — the wild donkeys quench their thirst.

12 Beside them the birds of the heavens dwell — they sing among the branches.

13 From your lofty abode you water the mountains — the earth is satisfied with the fruit of your work.

14 You cause the grass to grow for the livestock and plants for man to cultivate, that he may bring forth food from the earth

15 And wine to gladden the heart of man, oil to make his face shine and bread to strengthen man's heart.

16 The trees of YHWH are watered abundantly, the cedars of Lebanon that he planted.

17 In them the birds build their nests — the stork has her home in the fir trees.

18 The high mountains are for the wild goats — the rocks are a refuge for the rock badgers.

19 He made the moon to mark the seasons — the sun knows its time for setting.

20 You make darkness, and it is night, when all the beasts of the forest creep about.

21 The young lions roar for their prey, seeking their food from Creator.

22 When the sun rises, they steal away and lie down in their dens.

23 Man goes out to his work and to his labor until the evening.

24 O YHWH, how manifold are your works — in wisdom have you made them all — the earth is full of your creatures.

25 Here is the sea, great and wide, which teems with creatures innumerable, living things both small and great.

26 There go the ships, and Leviatan, which you formed to play in it.

27 These all look to you, to give them their food in due season.

28 When you give it to them, they gather it up — when you open your hand, they are filled with good things.

29 When you hide your face, they are dismayed — when you take away their breath, they die and return to their dust.

30 When you send forth your Spirit, they are created, and you renew the face of the ground.

31 May the glory of YHWH endure forever — may YHWH rejoice in his works.

32 Who looks on the earth and it trembles, who touches the mountains and they smoke.

33 I will sing to YHWH as long as I live — I will sing praise to my Creator while I have being.

34 May my meditation be pleasing to him, for I rejoice in YHWH.

35 Let sinners be consumed from the earth, and let the wicked be no more. Bless YHWH, O my soul. Praise YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 104 is attested in 2QPsa (2Q14) from Cave 2 and in 4QPsb (4Q84) from Cave 4. The psalm is the Tehillim's great creation hymn — a meditation on YHWH's providential ordering of the natural world that echoes Bereshit 1 while extending far beyond its scope. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 104 in full. The psalm is read as the Bereshit creation account sung in praise — each element of the natural world traced back to YHWH's direct sustaining action.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 104. The Cave 2 and Cave 4 manuscripts align with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 19 — he made the moon to mark the seasons. The Hebrew mo'adim — seasons — is the same word used for YHWH's appointed festivals in Vayikra 23. The moon does not merely mark time — it marks the covenant calendar. The Astronomical Book of Chanokh and the Qumran community's 364-day solar calendar both flow from this conviction that YHWH built his covenant calendar into the structure of creation. Verse 30 — when you send forth your Spirit, they are created, and you renew the face of the ground. YHWH's Spirit — the same Spirit that hovered over the waters in Bereshit 1:2 — is the ongoing agent of creation and renewal.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 105 Oh Give Thanks to YHWH — He Remembers His Covenant Forever — The Great History Psalm

1 Oh give thanks to YHWH — call upon his name — make known his deeds among the peoples.

2 Sing to him, sing praises to him — tell of all his wondrous works.

3 Glory in his holy name — let the hearts of those who seek YHWH rejoice.

4 Seek YHWH and his strength — seek his presence continually.

5 Remember the wondrous works that he has done, his miracles, and the judgments he uttered,

6 O offspring of Avraham, his servant, children of Yaakov, his chosen ones.

7 He is YHWH our Creator — his judgments are in all the earth.

8 He remembers his covenant forever, the word that he commanded, for a thousand generations,

9 The covenant that he made with Avraham, his sworn promise to Yitzhak,

10 Which he confirmed to Yaakov as a statute, to Yisra'EL as an everlasting covenant,

11 Saying, To you I will give the land of Kena'an as your portion for an inheritance.

12 When they were few in number, of little account, and sojourners in it,

13 Wandering from nation to nation, from one kingdom to another people,

14 He allowed no one to oppress them — he rebuked kings on their account,

15 Saying, Touch not my anointed ones, do my prophets no harm.

16 When he summoned a famine over the land and broke all supply of bread,

17 He had sent a man ahead of them — Yosef, who was sold as a slave.

18 His feet were hurt with fetters — his neck was put in a collar of iron.

19 Until what he had said came to pass, the word of YHWH tested him.

20 The king sent and released him — the ruler of the peoples set him free.

21 He made him lord of his house and ruler of all his possessions,

22 To bind his princes at his pleasure and to teach his elders wisdom.

23 Then Yisra'EL came to Egypt — Yaakov sojourned in the land of Ham.

24 And YHWH made his people very fruitful and made them stronger than their foes.

25 He turned their hearts to hate his people, to deal craftily with his servants.

26 He sent Moshe, his servant, and Aharon, whom he had chosen.

27 They performed his signs among them and miracles in the land of Ham.

28 He sent darkness, and made the land dark — they did not rebel against his words.

29 He turned their waters into blood and caused their fish to die.

30 Their land swarmed with frogs, even in the chambers of their kings.

31 He spoke, and there came swarms of flies, and gnats throughout their country.

32 He gave them hail for rain, and fiery lightning bolts through their land.

33 He struck down their vines and fig trees, and shattered the trees of their country.

34 He spoke, and the locusts came, young locusts without number,

35 Which devoured all the vegetation in their land and ate up the fruit of their ground.

36 He struck down all the firstborn in their land, the firstfruits of all their strength.

37 Then he brought out Yisra'EL with silver and gold, and there was none among his tribes who stumbled.

38 Egypt was glad when they departed, for dread of them had fallen upon it.

39 He spread a cloud for a covering, and fire to give light by night.

40 They asked, and he brought quail, and gave them bread from heaven in abundance.

41 He opened the rock, and water gushed out — it flowed through the desert like a river.

42 For he remembered his holy promise, and Avraham, his servant.

43 So he brought his people out with joy, his chosen ones with singing.

44 And he gave them the lands of the nations, and they took possession of the fruit of the peoples' toil,

45 That they might keep his statutes and observe his Torah. Praise YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 105 is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11 and also appears in 1 Divrei HaYamim 16:8-22 for verses 1-15. The Cave 11 manuscript is well preserved for this psalm. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 105 in full. The recitation of covenant history from Avraham through the Exodus is read as the foundational testimony every generation must know and transmit — the same command of Devarim 6:20-25.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] The parallel in 1 Divrei HaYamim 16:8-22 covers only verses 1-15 of Psalm 105, confirming that the full 45-verse psalm is the primary liturgical text of which the Divrei HaYamim passage is an excerpt. The DSS Cave 11 version aligns with the Masoretic reading.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 8 — he remembers his covenant forever, the word that he commanded, for a thousand generations. The covenant with Avraham was not a temporary arrangement — it was commanded for a thousand generations. The descendants of Avraham in every generation are heirs of the same covenant. Verse 42-43 — for he remembered his holy promise, and Avraham, his servant — so he brought his people out with joy. The Exodus is presented as YHWH's act of covenant memory: he remembered what he promised to Avraham and acted. Every subsequent act of deliverance is YHWH remembering the same covenant.

TEHILLIM — PSALM 106 Praise YHWH — Give Thanks to YHWH — He Saved Them for His Name's Sake — The Rebellion History

1 Praise YHWH — Oh give thanks to YHWH, for he is good, for his steadfast love endures forever.

2 Who can utter the mighty deeds of YHWH, or declare all his praise?

3 Blessed are they who observe justice, who do righteousness at all times.

4 Remember me, O YHWH, when you show favor to your people — help me when you save them,

5 That I may look upon the prosperity of your chosen ones, that I may rejoice in the gladness of your nation, that I may glory with your heritage.

6 Both we and our fathers have sinned — we have committed iniquity — we have done wickedness.

7 Our fathers, when they were in Egypt, did not consider your wondrous works — they did not remember the abundance of your steadfast love, but rebelled by the sea, at the Red Sea.

8 Yet he saved them for his name's sake, that he might make known his mighty power.

9 He rebuked the Red Sea, and it became dry, and he led them through the deep as through a desert.

10 So he saved them from the hand of the foe and redeemed them from the power of the enemy.

11 And the waters covered their adversaries — not one of them was left.

12 Then they believed his words — they sang his praise.

13 But they soon forgot his works — they did not wait for his counsel.

14 But they had a wanton craving in the wilderness, and put Creator to the test in the desert.

15 He gave them what they asked, but sent a wasting disease among them.

16 When men in the camp were jealous of Moshe and Aharon, the holy one of YHWH,

17 The earth opened and swallowed up Datan, and covered the company of Aviram.

18 Fire also broke out in their company — the flame burned up the wicked.

19 They made a calf in Horev and worshiped a metal image.

20 They exchanged the glory of Creator for the image of an ox that eats grass.

21 They forgot Creator, their Savior, who had done great things in Egypt,

22 Wondrous works in the land of Ham, and awesome deeds by the Red Sea.

23 Therefore he said he would destroy them — had not Moshe, his chosen one, stood in the breach before him, to turn away his wrath from destroying them.

24 Then they despised the pleasant land, having no faith in his promise.

25 They murmured in their tents, and did not obey the voice of YHWH.

26 Therefore he raised his hand and swore to them that he would make them fall in the wilderness,

27 And would make their offspring fall among the nations, scattering them among the lands.

28 Then they yoked themselves to the Baal of Peor, and ate sacrifices offered to the dead.

29 They provoked him to anger with their deeds, and a plague broke out among them.

30 Then Pinkhas stood up and intervened, and the plague was stayed.

31 And that was counted to him as righteousness from generation to generation forever.

32 They angered him at the waters of Merivah, and it went ill with Moshe on their account,

33 For they made his spirit bitter, and he spoke rashly with his lips.

34 They did not destroy the peoples, as YHWH commanded them,

35 But they mixed with the nations and learned to do as they did.

36 They served their idols, which became a snare to them.

37 They sacrificed their sons and their daughters to the demons.

38 They poured out innocent blood, the blood of their sons and daughters, whom they sacrificed to the idols of Kena'an, and the land was polluted with blood.

39 Thus they became unclean by their acts, and played the whore in their deeds.

40 Then the anger of YHWH was kindled against his people, and he abhorred his heritage.

41 He gave them into the hand of the nations, so that those who hated them ruled over them.

42 Their enemies oppressed them, and they were brought into subjection under their power.

43 Many times he delivered them, but they were rebellious in their purposes and were brought low through their iniquity.

44 Nevertheless, he looked upon their distress, when he heard their cry.

45 For their sake he remembered his covenant and relented according to the abundance of his steadfast love.

46 He caused them to be pitied by all those who held them captive.

47 Save us, O YHWH our Creator, and gather us from among the nations, that we may give thanks to your holy name and glory in your praise.

48 Blessed be YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, from everlasting to everlasting — and let all the people say, Amen. Praise YHWH.

COMPARATIVE MANUSCRIPT NOTES

[DSS] Psalm 106 closes Book IV of the Tehillim and is attested in 11QPsa (11Q5) from Cave 11. The closing doxology of verse 48 — Blessed be YHWH, the Creator of Yisra'EL, from everlasting to everlasting — Amen — matches the doxologies of Books I, II, and III, with the addition of Praise YHWH — Halelu-Yah. The text is consistent with the Masoretic in preserved portions.

[ORIT GE'EZ] The Orit Ge'ez preserves Psalm 106 in full. The sustained recitation of Yisra'EL's rebellion and YHWH's repeated covenant faithfulness — despite every provocation — is read as the definitive statement of YHWH's covenant patience.

[MASORETIC VARIANT] No significant variants from the DSS in Psalm 106. The Cave 11 manuscript aligns with the Masoretic reading. Verse 48 closes Book IV as the fourth of the five doxologies that conclude each book of the Tehillim.

[CRITICAL NOTE] Verse 45 — for their sake he remembered his covenant and relented according to the abundance of his steadfast love. After forty-four verses of rebellion — the calf at Horev, Baal Peor, the innocent blood of children, the whoring after idols — YHWH's response is not abandonment but covenant memory. He remembered his covenant. The Hebrew zakhar — remember — is the same covenant word used of Avraham in Bereshit, of Noakh in the flood, of the groaning Israelites in Egypt. YHWH's remembering is not passive recollection but active intervention. The covenant cannot be broken from YHWH's side.

END OF PART FIVE — PSALMS 90 THROUGH 106 — BOOK IV COMPLETE
